text.title: FLOW Multi-Frequency Monitoring Pattern
text.summary: FLOW is a multi-frequency autonomous monitoring pattern where the server monitors itself at increasing detail per cadence: hourly silent health checks (alert only on problems) and daily/weekly reports that always send.

rule.statement: The hourly check must be silent on success — only send an alert when problems are detected. Daily and weekly reports always send regardless of server state.
rule.rationale: An hourly alert that fires when everything is OK trains operators to ignore alerts. Silence = OK for the hourly check. For daily/weekly reports, always-send provides the baseline that makes deviations visible.

list.item: Hourly — quick health check (services, Docker, disk, memory, API) — silent on success
Daily — full status report (services, resources, errors, backup) — always sends
Weekly — trends (restarts, errors by day, disk growth) — always sends

example.note: Summarize runs 5 minutes after hourly to avoid race condition. Startup heartbeat has sleep 30 to wait for services to stabilize.

rule.statement: In the hourly script, collect issues into a variable. Only send a Telegram alert if the issues variable is non-empty. Log "All OK" on success for the log file, but send nothing externally.
rule.rationale: Alert fatigue is the failure mode of monitoring. Silent-on-success hourly checks ensure that when an alert does fire, operators treat it as actionable.

example.code:
ISSUES="" add_issue() { ISSUES="${ISSUES}- $1\n"; }
# Check services for svc in nero-core nero-web; do systemctl --user is-active --quiet "$svc" || add_issue "$svc is DOWN" done
# Check disk DISK_PCT=$(df / --output=pcent | tail -1 | tr -d ' %') [ "$DISK_PCT" -gt 90 ] && add_issue "Disk: ${DISK_PCT}%"
# Report only on problems if [ -n "$ISSUES" ]; then echo -e "FLOW ALERT $(hostname):\n$ISSUES" | /usr/local/bin/notify-telegram.sh log "ALERT: $(echo -e "$ISSUES" | tr '\n' ' ')" else log "All OK" fi

rule.statement: Add a logrotate rule for every new cron log file, or use a fixed-size log with truncation for low-value logs. Cron log files grow indefinitely otherwise.
rule.rationale: A backup script logging 5 lines per day produces ~1800 lines per year, manageable. An hourly health check logging 10 lines produces ~87,600 lines per year — logrotate is required.
